name: elphmod

metadata.name: elphmod
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v3 or later (GPLv3+)

documentation:
# Python modules for electron-phonon models

elphmod is a collection of Python modules to handle coupled tight-binding and
mass-spring models derived from first principles. It provides interfaces with
popular simulation software such as Quantum ESPRESSO, Wannier90, EPW, RESPACK,
and i-PI. It helps calculate dispersions, spectra, and response functions and
can be used to build and study distorted structures on supercells.

* `el` - tight-binding models from Wannier90
* `ph` - mass-spring models from Quantum ESPRESSO
* `elph` - electron-phonon coupling from EPW
* `elel` - Coulomb interaction from RESPACK
* `MPI` - work distribution and shared memory
* `bravais` - lattices, symmetries, and interpolation
* `dispersion` - diagonalization on paths and meshes
* `dos` - 2D tetrahedron methods
* `diagrams` - susceptibilities, self-energies, etc.
* `occupations` - step and delta smearing functions
* `md` - charge-density-wave dynamics using i-PI
* `eliashberg` - parameters for McMillan's formula
* `plot` - BZ plots, fatbands, etc.
* `misc` - constants, status bars, parsing, etc.
* `models` - nearest-neighbor models for testing

## Installation

You can install the latest version of elphmod from PyPI:

    python3 -m pip install elphmod

Or from the conda-forge channel on Anaconda Cloud:

    conda install conda-forge::elphmod

elphmod can optionally be run in parallel via MPI (with shared-memory support).
Using APT and pip, you can install the corresponding dependencies as follows:

    sudo apt install libopenmpi-dev
    python3 -m pip install mpi4py --no-binary=mpi4py

You can also download the complete repository, perform an editable installation,
and install the requirements of examples and documentation:

    git clone https://github.com/janberges/elphmod
    python3 -m pip install -e elphmod
    python3 -m pip install -r elphmod/examples/requirements.txt
    python3 -m pip install -r elphmod/doc/requirements.txt

## Documentation

The documentation can be found at <https://janberges.github.io/elphmod>.

Please also have a look at the examples directory.

## Reference

elphmod is stored on Zenodo: <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.5919991>.

functions.name: elphmod.occupations.hermite_polynomials
documentation:
Generate Hermite polynomials.

$$
H_0(x) &= 1 \\
H_1(x) &= 2 x \\
H_{n + 1}(x) &= 2 x H_n(x) - 2 n H_{n - 1}(x)
$$

functions.name: elphmod.occupations.methfessel_paxton_term
documentation:
Calculate Methfessel-Paxton term (without zeroth order).

From Phys. Rev. B 40, 3616 (1989):

$$
S_0(x) &= \frac {1 - erf(x)} 2 \\
S_N(x) &= S_0(x) + \sum_{n = 1}^N A_n H_{2 n - 1}(x) \exp(-x^2) \\
D_N(x) &= -S'(N, x) = \sum{n = 0}^N A_n H_{2 n}(x) \exp(-x^2) \\
A_n &= \frac{(-1)^n}{\sqrt \pi n! 4^n}
$$

This routine has been adapted from Quantum ESPRESSO:

- Step function: Modules/wgauss.f90
- Delta function: Modules/w0gauss.f90

functions.name: elphmod.occupations.lorentz
documentation:
Calculate Lorentz step function.

Used to simulate the influence of a wide box-shaped hybridization function
at low temperatures. Formula derived by Tim O. Wehling and Erik G.C.P. van
Loon. Here, we have $x = \epsilon / h$ with the height $h$ of
the hybridization, instead of $x = \epsilon / k T$ with the
temperature $T$.

# Parameters

smearing

  `lorentzian`, `lorentz`, and `heaviside`. If no string is passed,
  it is returned as is.

ignore

: Ignored keyword arguments, e.g., parameters from 'func'`read_pwi`.

# Returns

function

: Smearing function.

functions.name: elphmod.occupations.find_Fermi_level
documentation:
Determine chemical potential via fixed-point iteration.

See Eqs. 4.21 and 4.22 of https://janberges.de/theses/Master_Jan_Berges.pdf.

Parameters
----------
n : float
    Number of electrons (with spin) per unit cell.
e : ndarray
    Electronic energies for representative k points.
kT : float
    Smearing temperature.
f : function
    Electron distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.
mu : float
    Initial guess for chemical potential. By default, an estimate based on
    the assumption of a constant density of states is used.
tol : float
    Tolerance for the number of electrons.
eps : float
    Smallest allowed absolute value of divisor.

Returns
-------
float
    Chemical potential.

functions.name: elphmod.occupations.find_Fermi_level_simple
documentation:
Determine chemical potential via simple fixed-point iteration.

Parameters
----------
n : float
    Number of electrons (with spin) per unit cell.
e : ndarray
    Electronic energies for representative k points.
kT : float
    Smearing temperature.
f : function
    Electron distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.
mu : float
    Initial guess for chemical potential. By default, an estimate based on
    the assumption of a constant density of states is used.
tol : float
    Tolerance for the number of electrons.
damp : float
    Damping factor in the fixed-point equation. Large values may prevent
    convergence; small values will slow down convergence.

Returns
-------
float
    Chemical potential.

functions.name: elphmod.eliashberg.Tc
documentation:
Calculate critical temperature using McMillan's formula.

See Eqs. (2) and (34) by Allen and Dynes, Phys. Rev. B 12, 905 (1975).

Parameters
----------
lamda : float
    Effective electron-phonon coupling strength.
wlog : float
    Logarithmic average phonon energy in eV.
mustar : float
    Coulomb pseudopotential.
w2nd : bool, default None
    Second-moment average phonon energy used for shape correction in eV.
correct : bool, default False
    Apply Allen and Dynes' strong-coupling and, if `w2nd` is given, shape
    corrections of Eq. (34)?

Returns
-------
float
    Critical temperature in kelvin.

functions.name: elphmod.eliashberg.McMillan
documentation:
Calculate parameters and result of McMillan's formula.

# Parameters

nq

: Number of q points per dimension.

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

w2

: Squared phonon frequencies for irreducible q points.

g2

: Squared electron-phonon coupling (energy to the power of three).

eps

: Phonon frequencies squared below `eps` are set to `eps`; corresponding
  couplings are set to zero.

mustar

: Coulomb pseudopotential.

tetra

: Calculate double Fermi-surface average and density of states using 2D
  tetrahedron methods? Otherwise summations over broadened delta functions
  are performed.

kT

: Smearing temperature. Only used if `tetra` is `False`.

f

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`. Only used
  if `tetra` is `False`.

correct

: Apply Allen and Dynes' strong-coupling and shape corrections?

# Returns

float

: Effective electron-phonon coupling strength $\lambda$.

float

: Effective phonon frequency $\langle \omega \rangle$.

float

: Estimated critical temperature of superconductivity.

float, optional

: Second-moment average phonon energy used for shape correction.

methods.name: elphmod.md.Driver.superconductivity
documentation:
Calculate effective couplings and phonon frequencies.

Note that {attr}`d` is destroyed.

# Parameters

eps

: Phonon frequencies squared below `eps` are set to `eps`;
  corresponding couplings are set to zero.

tol

: If any phonon frequency squared is smaller than `tol`, all return
  values are `None`. A very small negative value can be chosen to
  skip calculations involving significant imaginary frequencies.

kT

: Smearing temperature. By default, {attr}`kT` is used.

# Returns

float

: Effective electron-phonon coupling strength $\lambda$.

float

: Logarithmic average phonon energy.

float

: Second-moment average phonon energy.

float

: Minimum phonon energy. Imaginary frequencies are given as negative.

methods.name: elphmod.md.Driver.__call__
documentation:
Interface driver with i-PI.

Parameters
----------
a : ndarray
    Dummy cell dimensions for variable-cell MD. Our cell is fixed.
r : ndarray
    Cartesian atomic positions.

Notes
-----
The factor 0.5 converts Rydberg to Hartree units.

MD driver for DFPT-based displacements dynamics.

# Parameters

elph

: Localized model for electron-phonon coupling. Initialize `el` with
  `rydberg=True` and `ph` and `elph` with `divide_mass=False` and
  map everthing to the appropriate supercell before (`elph.supercell`).

kT

: Smearing temperature in Ry.

f

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

n

: Number of electrons per primitive cell.

nk, nq

: Shape of k and q mesh. By default, only k = q = 0 is used.

supercell

: Supercell lattice vectors as multiples of primitive lattice vectors. If
  given, the simulation is performed on a supercell for q = k = 0. Sparse
  matrices are used for Hamiltonian, dynamical matrix, and electron-phonon
  coupling to save memory. Note that `elph` should belong to the primitive
  cell in this case, and `nq` and `nk` are only used for the unscreening,
  which is still done one the primitive cell. To do the unscreening on the
  supercell (recommended), a sparse supercell model `elph` can be directly
  provided instead of using this option.

unscreen

: Unscreen phonons? Otherwise, they are assumed to be unscreened already.

shared_memory

: Store {attr}`d0` and {attr}`d` in shared memory?

kwargs

: Attributes to be set initially.

# Attributes

elph, kT, f, n, nk, nq

: Copies of initialization parameters.

mu

: Current chemical potential.

k, q

: k and q meshes.

u

: Atomic displacements.

C0

: Unscreened force constants.

H0

: Unperturbed electron Hamiltonian in orbital basis.

d0

: Electron-phonon coupling in orbital basis.

d

: Electron-phonon coupling in band basis.

sparse

: Is the simulation performed on a supercell using sparse matrices?

interactive

: Shall plots be updated interactively?

scale

: Displacement scaling factor for plots.

size

: Marker size for atoms in points squared.

pause

: Minimal frame duration for interactive plots in seconds.

basis

: For each basis atom in the first primitive cell, indices of orbitals
  located at this atom. Matching atom and orbital orders as ensured by
  {meth}`elphmod.elph.Model.supercell` are required.

node, images

: Communicators between processes that share memory or same `node.rank`
  if `shared_memory`.

functions.name: elphmod.elph.sample
documentation:
Sample coupling for given q and k points and transform to band basis.

One purpose of this routine is full control of the complex phase.

# Parameters

g

: Electron-phonon coupling in the basis of electronic orbitals and
  Cartesian ionic displacements as a function of q and k in crystal
  coordinates with period $2 \pi$.

q

: List of q points in crystal coordinates $q_i \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

nk

: Number of k points per dimension, i.e., size of uniform mesh. Different
  numbers of k points along different axes can be specified via a tuple.
  Alternatively, `nk` is inferred from the shape of `U`.

U

: Electron eigenvectors for given k mesh.
  If present, transform from orbital to band basis.

u

documentation:
Calculate electron-phonon coupling for arbitary points k and k + q.

q1, q2, q2

  the Hamiltonian is diagonalized on the fly for each requested matrix
  element, this option lacks efficiency and control of complex phases.
  Consider the method `sample` of this class instead.

  the dynamical matrix is diagonalized on the fly for each requested

  on all but the first processor.

  Fourier transforms). Please note: To run this function serially,
  e.g., when parallelizing over q or k points, use `elphmod.MPI.I`.

  and transformed into the band basis.

methods.name: elphmod.elph.Model.to_epmatwp
documentation:
Save coupling to *.epmatwp* and *.wigner* files.

If {attr}`divide_ndegen`, the division by the degeneracies of the
Wigner-Seitz points is not undone before writing the *.epmatwp* file.
Instead, all degeneracies in the *.wiger* file are set to one.

# Parameters

prefix

: Filename stem.

Localized model for electron-phonon coupling.

# Parameters

epmatwp

: File with electron-phonon coupling in localized bases from EPW.

wigner

: File with definition of Wigner-Seitz supercells from EPW.

el

: Tight-binding model for the electrons.

ph

: Mass-spring model for the phonons.

Rk, Rg

: Lattice vectors of Wigner-Seitz supercells if `wigner` is omitted.

dk, dg

: Degeneracies of Wigner-Seitz points if `wigner` is omitted.

old_ws

: Use previous definition of Wigner-Seitz cells? This is required if
  `patches/qe-6.3-backports.patch` has been used.

divide_mass

: Divide electron-phonon coupling by square root of atomic masses?

divide_ndegen

: Divide real-space coupling by degeneracy of Wigner-Seitz point? Only
  `True` yields correct couplings. `False` should only be used for
  debugging.

shared_memory

: Read coupling from EPW into shared memory?

# Attributes

el

: Tight-binding model for the electrons.

ph

: Mass-spring model for the phonons.

Rk, Rg

: Lattice vectors $\vec R', \vec R$ of Wigner-Seitz supercells.

dk, dg

: Degeneracies of Wigner-Seitz points.

data

: Corresponding electron-phonon matrix elements in Ry{sup}`3/2`.

  $$
  g_{\vec R i \vec R' \alpha \beta} = \frac \hbar {\sqrt M_i}
      \bra{0 \alpha}
          \frac{\partial V}{\partial u_{\vec R i}}
      \ket{\vec R' \beta}
  $$

  If not {attr}`divide_mass`, the prefactor $\hbar / \sqrt{M_i}$ is
  absent and the units are Ry/bohr instead. If {attr}`ph.lr`, this is only
  the short-range component of the matrix elements.

divide_mass

: Has real-space coupling been divided by atomic masses?

divide_ndegen

: Has real-space coupling been divided by degeneracy of Wigner-Seitz
  point?

node, images

: Communicators between processes that share memory or same `node.rank`
  if `shared_memory`.

q

: Previously sampled q point, if any.

gq

: Rk-dependent coupling for above q point for possible reuse.

cells

: Lattice vectors of unit cells if the model describes a supercell.

g0

: Coupling on original q and k meshes.

Rk0

: Index of electronic lattice vector at origin.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.susceptibility
documentation:
Calculate real part of static electronic susceptibility.

$$
\chi_{\vec q} = \frac 2 N \sum_{\vec k} \frac
    {f(\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q}) - f(\epsilon_{\vec k})}
    {\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q} - \epsilon_{\vec k} + \I \eta}
$$

The resolution in q is limited by the resolution in k.

# Parameters

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

eta

: Absolute value of "infinitesimal" imaginary number in denominator.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

# Returns

function

: Static electronic susceptibility as a function of $q_1, q_2 \in
  [0, 2 \pi)$.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.susceptibility2
documentation:
Calculate the Lindhard bubble using the Green's functions explicitly.

$$
\chi = \frac \beta 4 - \frac 1 \beta
    \sum_n G(\nu_n) - \frac 1 {(\I \nu_n)^2}
$$

Only omega = 0 (static) calculation is performed.

For the treatment of the $1 / \I \nu_n$ tail, see:

> Appendix B of the thesis of Hartmut Hafermann.

Multiply by 2 for spin.

The resolution in q is limited by the resolution in k.

Original implementation by Erik G.C.P. van Loon.

# Parameters

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

nmats

: Number of fermionic Matsubara frequencies.

hyb_width

: Width of box-shaped hybridization function.

hyb_height

: Height of box-shaped hybridization function.

# Returns

function

: Static electronic susceptibility as a function of $q_1, q_2 \in
  [0, 2 \pi)$.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.polarization
documentation:
Calculate RPA polarization in orbital basis (density-density).

$$
\Pi_{\vec q \alpha \beta} = \frac 2 N \sum_{\vec k m n}
    \bracket{\vec k + \vec q \alpha}{\vec k + \vec q m}
    \bracket{\vec k n}{\vec k \alpha}
    \frac
        {f(\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m}) - f(\epsilon_{\vec k n})}
        {\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m} - \epsilon_{\vec k n}}
    \bracket{\vec k + \vec q m}{\vec k + \vec q \beta}
    \bracket{\vec k \beta}{\vec k n}
$$

The resolution in q is limited by the resolution in k.

If `subspace` is given, a cRPA calculation is performed. `subspace` must be
a boolean array with the same shape as `e`, where `True` marks states of
the target subspace, interactions between which are excluded.

# Parameters

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

U

: Coefficients for transform to orbital basis. These are given by the
  eigenvectors of the Wannier Hamiltonian.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

eps

: Smallest allowed absolute value of divisor.

subspace

: Boolean array to select k points and/or bands in cRPA target subspace.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

# Returns

function

: RPA polarization in orbital basis as a function of $q_1, q_2, q_3
  \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.phonon_self_energy
documentation:
Calculate phonon self-energy.

$$
\Pi_{\vec q \nu}(\omega) = \frac 2 N \sum_{\vec k m n}
    |g_{\vec q \nu \vec k m n}|^2 \frac
        {f(\epsilon_{\vec k n}) - f(\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m})}
        {\epsilon_{\vec k n} - \epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m} + \omega}
$$

# Parameters

q

: List of q points in crystal coordinates $q_i \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

g2

: Squared electron-phonon coupling. The resulting phonon self-energy will
  be a vector in the space of phonon modes.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

eps

: Smallest allowed absolute value of divisor.

omega

: Nonadiabatic frequency argument; shall include small imaginary
  regulator if nonzero.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

fluctuations

: Return integrand too (for fluctuation analysis)?

Delta

: Half the width of energy window around Fermi level to be excluded.

Delta_diff

: Calculate derivative of phonon self-energy w.r.t. `Delta`?

Delta_occupations

: Smoothened Heaviside function to realize excluded energy window.

Delta_kT

: Temperature to smoothen Heaviside function.

g

: Electron-phonon coupling on one side of the bubble. If given, `g2` is
  ignored. The resulting phonon self-energy will be a matrix (an outer
  product of `g` and `G`) in the space of phonon modes or displacements.

G

: Electron-phonon coupling on the other side of the bubble. If absent, `g`
  is used.

symmetrize

: Symmetrize phonon self-energy with respect to swapping `g` and `G`?

diagonal

: Neglect off-diagonal elements when using `g` and `G`?

U

: Contact interaction (a matrix in band indices) to model the effect of
  excitons. Only used if `g` is present. Associated terms are currently
  excluded from the integrand provided for fluctuation diagnostics.

# Returns

ndarray

: Phonon self-energy.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.phonon_self_energy_fermi_shift
documentation:
Calculate phonon self-energy arising from change of chemical potential.

associated with a possible change of the chemical potential has to be added.

# Parameters

e

g

: Electron-phonon coupling for $\vec q = 0$.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

# Returns

ndarray

: Phonon self-energy correction.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.renormalize_coupling_band
documentation:
Calculate renormalized electron-phonon coupling in band basis.

$$
\tilde g_{\vec q \nu \vec k m n} = g_{\vec q \nu \vec k m n}
    + \frac 2 N \sum_{\vec k' m' n' \alpha \beta \gamma \delta}
    U_{\vec k + \vec q \alpha m}^* U_{\vec k \beta n}
    W_{\vec q \alpha \beta \gamma \delta}
    U_{\vec k' + \vec q \gamma m'} U_{\vec k' \delta n'}^*
    \frac
        {f(\epsilon_{\vec k' + \vec q m'}) - f(\epsilon_{\vec k' n'})}
        {\epsilon_{\vec k' + \vec q m'} - \epsilon_{\vec k' n'}}
    g_{\vec q \nu \vec k' m' n'}
$$

# Parameters

q

: List of q points in crystal coordinates $q_i \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

g

: Bare electron-phonon coupling in band basis.

W

: Dressed q-dependent Coulomb interaction in orbital basis.

U

: Eigenvectors of Wannier Hamiltonian belonging to considered bands.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

eps

: Smallest allowed absolute value of divisor.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

nbnd_sub

: Number of bands for Lindhard bubble. Defaults to all bands.

status

: Print status messages during the calculation?

# Returns

ndarray

: Dressed electron-phonon coupling in band basis.

# See Also

renormalize_coupling_orbital

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.renormalize_coupling_orbital
documentation:
Calculate renormalized electron-phonon coupling in orbital basis.

$$
\tilde g_{\vec q \nu \vec k \alpha \beta}
    = g_{\vec q \nu \vec k \alpha \beta}
    + \frac 2 N \sum_{\vec k m n \alpha' \beta' \gamma \delta}
    W_{\vec q \alpha \beta \gamma \delta}
    U_{\vec k + \vec q \gamma m} U_{\vec k \delta n}^*
    \frac
        {f(\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m}) - f(\epsilon_{\vec k n})}
        {\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m} - \epsilon_{\vec k n}}
    U_{\vec k + \vec q \alpha' m}^* U_{\vec k \beta' n}
    g_{\vec q \nu \vec k \alpha' \beta'}
$$

# Parameters

q

: List of q points in crystal coordinates $q_i \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

g

: Bare electron-phonon coupling in orbital basis.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.double_fermi_surface_average
documentation:
Calculate double Fermi-surface average.

Please note that not the average itself is returned!

$$
\langle g^2 \rangle = \frac {
    \sum_{\vec q \nu \vec k m n}
    |g_{\vec q \nu \vec k m n}|^2
    \delta(\epsilon_{\vec k n})
    \delta(\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m})
}{
    \sum_{\vec q \vec k m n}
    \delta(\epsilon_{\vec k n})
    \delta(\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q m})
}
$$

# Parameters

q

: List of q points in crystal coordinates $q_i \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

g2

: Quantity to be averaged, typically squared electron-phonon coupling.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

# Returns

ndarray

: Enumerator of double Fermi-surface average before $\vec q \nu$
  summation.

ndarray

: Denominator of double Fermi-surface average before $\vec q$
  summation.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.triangle
documentation:
Calculate triangle diagram (third order of grand potential).

$$
\chi_{\vec q \mu \vec q' \nu}
    &= \sum_{\vec k \alpha \beta \gamma} \frac
    {
        \epsilon_{\vec k \alpha}
            (f_{\vec k + \vec q \beta} - f_{\vec k + \vec q' \gamma})
        + \epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q \beta}
            (f_{\vec k + \vec q' \gamma} - f_{\vec k \alpha})
        + \epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q' \gamma}
            (f_{\vec k \alpha} - f_{\vec k + \vec q \beta})
    }{
        (\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q \beta}
            - \epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q' \gamma})
        (\epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q' \gamma}
            - \epsilon_{\vec k \alpha})
        (\epsilon_{\vec k \alpha}
            - \epsilon_{\vec k + \vec q \beta})
    }
    \\ &\times
    g^*_{\vec q \mu \vec k \beta \alpha}
    g_{\vec q' \nu \vec k \gamma \alpha}
    g_{\vec q - \vec q' \nu \vec k + \vec q' \beta \gamma}
$$

```text
           q u
            o
           / \
          /   \
         /     \
   k a  v       ^  k+q b
       /         \
      /           \
     /             \
    o------->-------o
q' v     k+q' c      q-q' v
```

# Parameters

q, Q

: q points in crystal coordinates $q_i, q'_i \in [0, 2 \pi)$.

e

: Electron dispersion on uniform mesh. The Fermi level must be at zero.

gq, gQ, gqQ

: Electron-phonon coupling for given q points and their difference.

kT

: Smearing temperature.

eps

: Smallest allowed absolute value of divisor.

occupations

: Particle distribution as a function of energy divided by `kT`.

fluctuations

: Return integrand too (for fluctuation analysis)?

# Returns

complex

: Value of triangle.

functions.name: elphmod.diagrams.green_kubo_conductivity
documentation:
Calculate Green-Kubo optical conductivity (to be tested).

See Eq. (8) by Abramovitch et al., Phys. Rev. Mater. 7, 093801 (2023).
Note that we have omitted the division by the unit-cell volume.

Parameters
----------
v : ndarray
    Fermi velocity on uniform mesh.
A : ndarray
    Electronic spectral function.
omega : ndarray

functions.name: elphmod.dispersion.dispersion
documentation:
Diagonalize Hamiltonian or dynamical matrix for given k points.

# Parameters

matrix

: Matrix to be diagonalized as a function of k in crystal coordinates with
  period $2 \pi$.

k

: k points in crystal coordinates with period $2 \pi$. Wave-vector
  components are stored along the last axis; the leading axes describe
  the mesh. The shapes of `matrix` and `k` together determine the shape
  of the results.

angle

: Angle between the axes of the reciprocal lattice.

vectors

: Return eigenvectors?

gauge

: Choose largest element of each eigenvector to be real? Not stable!

rotate

: Align (phonon) eigenvectors with wave vector k via in-plane rotation.
  This is experimental and supposed to support the band-order algorithm.

order

: Order/disentangle bands via their k-local character? Depending on the
  topology of the band structure, this may not be possible. Adjacent
  points in `k` must be adjacent in the Brillouin zone too.

hermitian

: Assume `matrix` to be Hermitian, i.e., calculate real eigenvalues?

broadcast

: Broadcast result from rank 0 to all processes?

shared_memory

: Store results in shared memory?

order_kwargs

: Keyword arguments passed to {func}`band_order`.

# Returns

ndarray

: Eigenvalues for the given k points.

ndarray, optional

: Corresponding eigenvectors.

ndarray, optional

: Indices which have been used to order the bands.

functions.name: elphmod.dispersion.unfolding_weights
documentation:
Calculate weights for "unfolding" of supercell dispersions.

# Parameters

k

: k points in arbitrary representation.

R

: Positions of the unit cells in the supercell. The representation must
  be compatible with the k points: If `k` is given in crystal coordinates
  with a period of $2 \pi$, `R` must be given in crystal
  coordinates with a period of 1. `k` and `R` can also be defined in
  Cartesian coordinates.

U0: ndarray

: Eigenvectors of the symmetric system.

U: ndarray

: Eigenvectors of the supercell system.

blocks0

: Mapping from indices of `R` to slices of `U0`. By default, all orbitals
  are selected.

blocks

: Mapping from indices of `R` to slices of `U`. By default, it is assumed
  that the orbitals are grouped into blocks corresponding to unit cells.
  Within the blocks, the order of the orbitals is as in `U0`. The order
  of the blocks is as in `R`.

sgn

: Sign convention for Fourier transform in tight-binding model.
  The default sign `-1` is suitable for data from Wannier90 as provided
  by {meth}`elphmod.el.Model.H`.
  [Other conventions](https://doi.org/10.26092/elib/250) require `+1`.

# Returns

ndarray

: Weights of the supercell states.

functions.name: elphmod.el.proj_sum
documentation:
Sum over selected atomic projections.

Example:

```python
proj = read_atomic_projections('atomic_proj.xml')
orbitals = read_projwf_out('projwfc.out')
proj = proj_sum(proj, orbitals, 'S-p', 'Ta-d{z2, x2-y2, xy}')
```

proj

orbitals

: Names of all orbitals from {func}`read_projwfc_out`.

groups

: Comma-separated lists of names of selected orbitals. Omitted name
  components are summed over. Curly braces are expanded.

  "other" orbitals from {func}`read_atomic_projections`, add the name
  `x` to `orbitals` instead.

functions.name: elphmod.el.eband
documentation:
Calculate `eband` part of one-electron energy.

The 'one-electron contribution' energy in the Quantum ESPRESSO PWscf output
is a sum `eband + deband`. Here, we can calculate the `eband` part.

To compare it with the Quantum ESPRESSO result, you need to modify
the `SUBROUTINE print_energies ( printout )` from *electrons.f90*.

Change:

```
WRITE( stdout, 9062 ) (eband + deband), ehart, ( etxc - etxcc ), ewld
```

to:

```
WRITE( stdout, 9062 ) eband, &
   (eband + deband), ehart, ( etxc - etxcc ), ewld
```

and:

```
9062 FORMAT( '     one-electron contribution =',F17.8,' Ry' &
```

to:

```
9062 FORMAT( '     sum bands                 =',F17.8,' Ry' &
            /'     one-electron contribution =',F17.8,' Ry' &
```

# Parameters

pw_scf_out

: Name of the output file (typically `'pw.out'` or `'scf.out'`).

# Returns

eband

: The band energy.

functions.name: elphmod.el.read_pp_density
documentation:
Read file *filout* with charge density generated by `pp.x`.

Calculate total charge

$$
Q = \int \rho(\vec r) dx dy dz =
    = \frac \Omega {n_1 n_2 n_3} \sum_{i j k} \rho_{i j k},
$$

where $\Omega$ is the unit-cell volume, $n_r$ are the FFT grid
dimensions, and $i, j, k$ run over FFT real-space grid points.

# Parameters

filename

: Filename.

# Returns

rho

: Electronic charge density $\rho_{i j k}$ on FFT real-space grid
  points.

tot_charge: float

: Total charge calculated from charge density $\rho_{i j k}$.

ibrav

a, b, c

  points.

: Number of reciprocal lattice vectors.

functions.name: elphmod.el.read_wfc
documentation:
Read the wave function output from Quantum ESPRESSO.

    c_{n, \vec k + \vec G} \E^{\I (\vec k + \vec G) \vec r}
$$

methods.name: elphmod.el.Model.standardize

Standardize tight-binding data.

- Keep only nonzero hopping matrices.
- Sum over repeated lattice vectors.
- Sort lattice vectors.

$$

H_{\vec R} = H_{-\vec R}^\dagger
$$

eps

: Threshold for "nonzero" matrix elements in units of the maximum
  matrix element.

symmetrize

: Symmetrize hopping?

methods.name: elphmod.el.Model.to_hrdat
documentation:
Save tight-binding model to *_hr.dat* file.

Parameters
----------
seedname : str
    Common prefix of Wannier90 input and output files.

Tight-binding model for the electrons.

# Parameters

seedname

: Common prefix of Wannier90 output files: *seedname_hr.dat* with the
  Hamiltonian in the Wannier basis and, optionally, *seedname_wsvec.dat*
  with superlattice vectors used to symmetrize the long-range hopping.
  Alternatively, *dat.h_mat_r* from RESPACK can be used.

N

: Numbers of unit cells per direction on which RESPACK data is defined.
  This can be omitted if all numbers are even.

a

: Bravais lattice vectors used to map RESPACK data to Wigner-Seitz cell.
  By default, a cubic cell is assumed.

r

: Positions of orbital centers used to map RESPACK data to Wigner-Seitz
  cell. By default, all orbitals are assumed to be located at the origin
  of the unit cell.

divide_ndegen

: Divide hopping by degeneracy of Wigner-Seitz point and apply the
  abovementioned correction? Only `True` yields correct bands.
  `False` is only used in combination with {func}`decayH`.

read_xsf

: Read Wannier functions in position representation in the XCrySDen
  structure format (XSF)?

normalize_wf

: Normalize Wannier functions? This is only recommended if a single and
  complete image of each Wannier function is contained in the supercell
  written to the XSF file. This may not be the case for a small number of
  k points along one direction (e.g., a single k point in the out-of-plane
  direction in the case of 2D materials), especially in combination with a
  large supercell.

buffer_wf

: After reading XSF files, store Wannier functions and corresponding
  real-space mesh in binary files, which will be read next time? Since
  reading the XSF files is slow, this can save a lot of time for large
  systems. Make sure to delete the binary files *seedname_wf.npy* and
  *seedname_xyz.npy* whensoever the XSF files change.

check_ortho

: Check if Wannier functions are orthogonal?

rydberg

: Convert energies from eV to Ry?

shared_memory

: Store Wannier functions in shared memory?

# Attributes

R

: Lattice vectors $\vec R$ of Wigner-Seitz supercell.

data

: Corresponding on-site energies and hoppings in eV.

  $$
  H_{\vec R \alpha \beta} = \bra{0 \alpha} H \ket{\vec R \beta}
  $$

  If {attr}`rydberg` is `True`, the units are Ry instead.

size

: Number of Wannier functions/bands.

nk

: Guessed shape of original k-point mesh.

cells

: Lattice vectors of unit cells if the model describes a supercell.

N

: Primitive vectors of supercell if the model describes a supercell.

W

: Wannier functions in position representation if `read_xsf`.

r

: Cartesian positions belonging to Wannier functions if `read_xsf`.

tau

: Positions of basis atoms if `read_xsf`.

atom_order

: Ordered list of atoms if `read_xsf`.

dV

: Volume element/voxel volume belonging to `r` if `read_xsf`.

divide_ndegen

: Have hoppings been divided by degeneracy of Wigner-Seitz point?

rydberg

: Have energies been converted from eV to Ry?

functions.name: elphmod.MPI.shm_split
documentation:
Create communicators for use with shared memory.

# Parameters

comm

: Overarching communicator.

shared_memory

: Use shared memory? Provided for convenience.

# Returns

node

: Communicator between processes that share memory (on the same node).

images

: Communicator between processes that have the same `node.rank`.

# Warnings

If shared memory is not implemented, each process shares memory only with
itself. A warning is issued.

# Notes

Visualization for a machine with 2 nodes with 4 processors each:

```
 ________________ ________________ ________________ ________________
| comm.rank: 0   | comm.rank: 1   | comm.rank: 2   | comm.rank: 3   |
| node.rank: 0   | node.rank: 1   | node.rank: 2   | node.rank: 3   |
| images.rank: 0 | images.rank: 0 | images.rank: 0 | images.rank: 0 |
|________________|________________|________________|________________|
 ________________ ________________ ________________ ________________
| comm.rank: 4   | comm.rank: 5   | comm.rank: 6   | comm.rank: 7   |
| node.rank: 0   | node.rank: 1   | node.rank: 2   | node.rank: 3   |
| images.rank: 1 | images.rank: 1 | images.rank: 1 | images.rank: 1 |
|________________|________________|________________|________________|
```

Since both `node.rank` and `images.rank` are sorted by `comm.rank`,
`comm.rank == 0` is equivalent to `node.rank == images.rank == 0`.

functions.name: elphmod.MPI.shared_array
documentation:
Create array whose memory is shared among all processes on same node.

With `shared_memory=False` (`single_memory=True`) a conventional array
is created on each (only one) processor, which however allows for the same
broadcasting syntax as shown below.

```python
# Set up huge array:
node, images, array = shared_array(2 ** 30, dtype=np.uint8)

# Write data on one node:
if comm.rank == 0:
    array[:] = 0

# Broadcast data to other nodes:
if node.rank == 0:
    images.Bcast(array)

# Wait if node.rank != 0:
comm.Barrier()
```

functions.name: elphmod.MPI.elphmodenv
documentation:
Print commands to change number of threads.

Run `eval $(elphmodenv)` before your Python scripts to prevent NumPy from
using multiple processors for linear algebra. This is advantageous when
parallelizing with MPI already (`mpirun python3 script.py`) or on shared
servers, where CPU resources must be used with restraint.

Combine `eval $(elphmodenv X)` with `mpirun -n Y python3 script.py`, where
the product of X an Y is the number of available CPUs, for hybrid
parallelization.

functions.name: elphmod.ph.q2r
documentation:
Interpolate dynamical matrices given for irreducible wedge of q points.

This function replaces `interpolate_dynamical_matrices`, which depends on
Quantum ESPRESSO. For 2D lattices, it is sufficient to provide dynamical
matrices `D_irr` for the irreducible q points `q_irr`. Here, for the square
lattice, the rotation symmetry (90 degrees) is currently disabled! In turn,
for 1D and 2D lattices, dynamical matrices `D_full` on the complete uniform
q-point mesh must be given.

# Parameters

ph

: Mass-spring model.

D_irr

: Dynamical matrices for all irreducible q points.

q_irr

: Irreducible q points in crystal coordinates with period $2 \pi$.

nq

: Number of q points per dimension, i.e., size of uniform mesh. Different
  numbers of q points along different axes can be specified via a tuple.
  Alternatively, `nq` is inferred from the shape of `D_full`.

D_full

: Dynamical matrices on complete uniform q-point mesh.

angle

: Angle between mesh axes in degrees.

apply_asr

: Enforce acoustic sum rule by overwriting self force constants?

apply_asr_simple

: Apply simple acoustic sum rule correction to force constants? This sets
  the self force constant to minus the sum of all other force constants.

apply_rsr

: Enforce rotation sum rule by overwriting self force constants?

flfrc

: Filename. If given, save file with force constants as generated by
  `q2r.x`. This is done before acoustic and rotation sum are applied.

divide_mass

: Have input dynamical matrices been divided by atomic mass? This is
  independent of `ph.divide_mass`, which is always respected.

functions.name: elphmod.ph.interpolate_dynamical_matrices
documentation:
Interpolate dynamical matrices given for irreducible wedge of q points.

This function still uses the Quantum ESPRESSO executables `q2qstar.x` and
`q2r.x`.  They are called in serial by each MPI process, which leads to
problems if they have been compiled for parallel execution. If you want to
run this function in parallel, you have two choices:

> 1. Configure Quantum ESPRESSO for compilation of serial executables
>    via `./configure --disable-parallel` and run `make ph`. If you
>    do not want to make them available through the environmental
>    variable `PATH`, you can also set the parameter `qe-prefix` to
>    `'/path/to/serial/q-e/bin/'`.  The trailing slash is required.
> 2. If your MPI implementation supports nested calls to `mpirun`, you
>    may try to set `qe_prefix` to `'mpirun -np 1 '`. The trailing
>    space is required.

# Parameters

D

: Dynamical matrices for all irreducible q points.

q

: Irreducible q points in crystal coordinates with period $2 \pi$.

nq

: Number of q points per dimension, i.e., size of uniform mesh.

fildyn_template

: Complete name of *fildyn* file from which to take header information.

fildyn

: Prefix for written files with dynamical matrices.

flfrc

: Name of written file with interatomic force constants.

write_fildyn0

: Write *fildyn0* needed by `q2r.x`? Otherwise the file must be present.

apply_asr

: Enforce acoustic sum rule by overwriting self force constants?

apply_asr_simple

: Apply simple acoustic sum rule correction to force constants? This sets
  the self force constant to minus the sum of all other force constants.

apply_rsr

: Enforce rotation sum rule by overwriting self force constants?

divide_mass

: Have input dynamical matrices been divided by atomic mass? This is
  independent of `ph.divide_mass`, which is always respected.

qe_prefix

: String to prepend to names of Quantum ESPRESSO executables.

clean

: Delete all temporary files afterwards?

# Returns

function

: Fourier-interpolant (via force constants) for dynamical matrices.

functions.name: elphmod.ph.spectral_function
documentation:
Calculate phonon spectral function.

See Eq. 76 by Monacelli et al., J. Phys. Condens. Matter 33, 363001 (2021).
We use an additional prefactor of 2 to normalize the frequency integral
(from zero to infinity) of the phonon spectral function to the number of
modes (for each q point).

Parameters
----------
D : ndarray
    Dynamical matrix. The last three axes belong to the two displacement
    directions and the frequency argument.
omega : ndarray
    Frequency arguments.
eta : float
    Broadening parameter for all phonon modes.

Returns
-------
ndarray
    Spectral function. The first axes belongs to the frequency argument.

methods.name: elphmod.ph.Model.to_flfrc
documentation:
Save mass-spring model to force-constants file.

# Parameters

flfrc

: Filename.

nr1, nr2, nr3

: Mesh dimensions. If not given, {attr}`nq` is assumed.

Mass-spring model for the phonons.

# Parameters

flfrc

: File with interatomic force constants from `q2r.x` or common filename
  part (without appended/inserted q-point number) of dynamical matrices
  from `ph.x`. Both the plain-text and XML format are supported.

quadrupole_fmt

: File with quadrupole tensors in format suitable for `epw.x`.

apply_asr

: Apply acoustic sum rule correction to force constants?

apply_asr_simple

: Apply simple acoustic sum rule correction to force constants? This sets
  the self force constant to minus the sum of all other force constants.

apply_zasr

: Apply acoustic sum rule correction to Born effective charges?

apply_rsr

: Apply rotation sum rule correction to force constants?

lr

: Compute long-range terms in case of polar material?

lr2d

: Compute long-range terms for two-dimensional system if `lr`?

amass

: Atomic masses if `flfrc` is omitted.

at

: Bravais lattice vectors if `flfrc` is omitted.

tau

: Positions of basis atoms if `flfrc` is omitted.

atom_order

: Ordered list of atoms if `flfrc` is omitted.

alph

: Ewald parameter if `flfrc` is omitted.

epsil

: Dielectric tensor if `flfrc` is omitted.

zeu

: Born effective charges if `flfrc` is omitted.

Q

: Quadrupole tensors if `quadrupole_fmt` is omitted.

L

: Range-separation parameter for two-dimensional electrostatics.

perp

: Yield out-of-plane long-range terms if `L` is nonzero?

divide_mass

: Divide force constants and Born effective charges by atomic masses?

divide_ndegen

: Divide force constants by degeneracy of Wigner-Seitz point? Only
  `True` yields correct phonons. `False` should only be used for
  debugging.

ifc

: Name of file with interatomic force constants to be created if `flfrc`
  is prefix of files with dynamical matrices. Used to emulate `q2r.x`.

# Attributes

R

: Lattice vectors $\vec R$ of Wigner-Seitz supercell.

data

: Corresponding force constants divided by atomic masses in Ry{sup}`2`.

  $$
  D_{\vec R i j} = \frac{\hbar^2}{\sqrt{M_i M_j}}
      \frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial u_{0 i} \partial u_{\vec R j}}
  $$

  If not {attr}`divide_mass`, the prefactor $\hbar^2 / \sqrt{M_i
  M_j}$ is absent and the units are Ry/bohr{sup}`2` instead. If
  {attr}`lr`, this is only the short-range component of the force
  constants.

M

: Atomic masses $M_i$.

a

: Bravais lattice vectors.

r

: Positions of basis atoms.

l

: Bond lengths.

atom_order

: Ordered list of atoms.

alpha

: Ewald parameter.

eps

: Dielectric tensor.

Z

: Born effective charges.

z

: Born effective charges divided by square root of atomic masses.

Q

: Quadrupole tensors.

q

: Quadrupole tensors divided by square root of atomic masses.

L

: Range-separation parameter for two-dimensional electrostatics.

perp

: Yield out-of-plane long-range terms if `L` is nonzero?

divide_mass

: Have force constants and Born charges been divided by atomic masses?

divide_ndegen

: Have force constants been divided by degeneracy of Wigner-Seitz point?

size

: Number of displacement directions/bands.

nat

: Number of atoms.

nq

: Shape of original q-point mesh.

q0

: Original q-point mesh.

D0

: Dynamical matrices on original q-point mesh.

cells

: Lattice vectors of unit cells if the model describes a supercell.

N

: Primitive vectors of supercell if the model describes a supercell.

lr

: Compute long-range terms?

lr2d

: Compute long-range terms for two-dimensional system if `lr`?

b

: Reciprocal primitive vectors if `lr`.

prefactor

: Prefactor of long-range terms if `lr`.

r_eff

: Effective thickness if `lr2d`.

scale

: Relevant scaling factor if `lr`.

D0_lr

: Constant part of long-range correction to dynamical matrix if `lr`.

functions.name: elphmod.dos.hexDOS
documentation:
Calculate DOS from energies on triangular mesh (2D tetrahedron method).

# Parameters

energies

: Energies on triangular mesh.

minimum, maximum

: Energy window of interest (for efficiency).

# Returns

function

: Density of states as a function of energy.

# Notes

Integration over all energies yields unity.

Derivation: The density of states can be expressed as

$$
\rho(E) = \frac 1 V \int_{W(k) = E} \frac{\D k}{|\vec \nabla W(k)|},
$$

where $V$ is the volume of a reciprocal unit cell, $W(k)$ is
the dispersion relation and the integral is over an energy isosurface/line
in k space.

In the following, consider a two dimensional reciprocal unit cell which can
be divided into $2 \times N \times N$ equilateral triangles of
reciprocal side length $a$, on each of which the energy is
interpolated linearly. On a triangle with the energies $A, B, C$ at
its corners, the gradient of the energy plane is

$$
|\vec \nabla W| = \frac 2 {\sqrt 3 a}
    \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2 - A B - A C - B C}.
$$

For the special case $A < B < E < C$, the reciprocal length of the
$E$ isoline within the triangle reads

$$
\D k = a \sqrt{A^2 + B^2 + C^2 - A B - A C - B C}
    \frac{C - E}{(C - A) (C - B)}.
$$

Taking into account that $V = N^2 a^2 \sqrt 3 / 2$, one finds the
contribution of this triangle to the density of states:

$$
\frac 1 {N^2} \frac{C - E}{(C - A) (C - B)}.
$$

functions.name: elphmod.dos.hexa2F
documentation:
Calculate $\alpha^2 F$ from energies and coupling.

# Parameters

energies

: Energies on triangular mesh.

couplings

: Couplings on triangular mesh.

minimum, maximum

: Energy window of interest (for efficiency).

# Returns

function

: $\alpha^2 F$ as a function of energy.

# Notes

Integration over all energies yields the arithmetic mean of the coupling.

Note that it may be more convenient to calculate the mass renormalization

$$
\lambda_n = \int_0^\infty \D \omega     \frac{2 \omega \alpha^2 F(\omega)}         {\omega^2 + \omega_n^2}     = N(0) \sum_{\vec q \nu}     \frac{2 \omega_{\vec q \nu} g^2_{\vec q \nu}}         {\omega_{\vec q \nu}^2 + \omega_n^2}
$$

directly from energies and couplings, without integrating this function.

functions.name: elphmod.dos.double_delta
documentation:
Calculate double-delta integrals via 2D tetrahedron method .

$$
I(z) = \frac 1 N \sum_{\vec k}     \delta(x_{\vec k} - z) \delta(y_{\vec k} - z) f_{\vec k}
$$

# Parameters

x, y, f

: Three functions sampled on uniform N x N mesh.

eps

: Negligible difference between fractional mesh-point indices.

# Returns

function

: Intersection points of $x, y = z$ isolines and corresponding
  weights as a function of $z$. The above double-delta integral
  $I(z)$ can be calculated as:

  ```python
  I_z = sum(double_delta(x, y, f)(z).values())
  ```

Minimize distance of points on periodic axis via full-period shifts.

```python
>>> stack(3, 5, 9, 12, period=10)
[13, 15, 9, 12]
```

```text
 In: ... | ox x   x| xo o   o| oo o   o| ...
Out: ... | oo o   x| xx x   o| oo o   o| ...
```

# Parameters

points

: Points on periodic axis.

period

: Period of axis. Specified via `**kwargs` for Python-2 compatibility.

# Returns

ndarray

: Points equivalent to input, with minimal distance on non-periodic axis.

functions.name: elphmod.bravais.linear_interpolation
documentation:
Perform linear interpolation in one or two dimensions.

The edges are interpolated using periodic boundary conditions.

# Parameters

data

: Data on uniform 1D or 2D (triangular or rectangular) lattice.

angle

: Angle between lattice vectors in degrees.

axes

: Axes of `data` along which to interpolate (lattice vectors).

period

: If the values of `data` are defined on a periodic axis (i.e., only with
  respect to the modulo operation), the period of this axis. This is used
  in combination with `stack` to always interpolate across the shortest
  distance of two neighboring points.

polar

: Interpolate complex values linearly in polar coordinates? This is
  helpful if neighboring data points have arbitrary complex phases.

# Returns

function

: Interpolant for `data`. `linear_interpolation(data)(i, j)` yields the
  same value as `data[i, j]`. Thus the data array is "generalized" with
  respect to fractional indices.

# See Also

stack : Condense point cloud on periodic axis.
resize : Compress or stretch data via linear interpolation.
Fourier_interpolation : Alternative interpolation routine.
